message.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>The performance of hybridization capture combined with next‐generation sequencing (<jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">NGS</jats:styled-content>) has seen limited investigation with samples from hot and arid regions until now. We applied hybridization capture and shotgun sequencing to recover <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> sequences from bone specimens of ancient‐domestic dromedary (<jats:italic>Camelus dromedarius</jats:italic>) and its extinct ancestor, the wild dromedary from Jordan, Syria, Turkey and the Arabian Peninsula, respectively. Our results show that hybridization capture increased the percentage of mitochondrial <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> (mt<jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content>) recovery by an average 187‐fold and in some cases yielded virtually complete mitochondrial (mt) genomes at multifold coverage in a single capture experiment. Furthermore, we tested the effect of hybridization temperature and time by using a touchdown approach on a limited number of samples. We observed no significant difference in the number of unique dromedary mt<jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> reads retrieved with the standard capture compared to the touchdown method. In total, we obtained 14 partial mitochondrial genomes from ancient‐domestic dromedaries with 17–95% length coverage and 1.27–47.1‐fold read depths for the covered regions. Using whole‐genome shotgun sequencing, we successfully recovered endogenous dromedary nuclear <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> (nu<jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content>) from domestic and wild dromedary specimens with 1–1.06‐fold read depths for covered regions. Our results highlight that despite recent methodological advances, obtaining ancient <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> (<jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">aDNA</jats:styled-content>) from specimens recovered from hot, arid environments is still problematic. Hybridization protocols require specific optimization, and samples at the limit of <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> preservation need multiple replications of <jats:styled-content style="fixed-case">DNA</jats:styled-content> extraction and hybridization capture as has been shown previously for Middle Pleistocene specimens.</jats:p>
title: Combined hybridization capture and shotgun sequencing for ancient <scp>DNA</scp> analysis of extinct wild and domestic dromedary camel
